entry.title: Uyuni salt flats
entry.content:
Today was the highlight of the trip — we finally got to see the salt flats. We left at 4:30 a.m. to catch the sunrise, and it was absolutely worth it.

When we arrived, part of the flats was covered by a thin layer of rainwater, creating a magical view. During the rainy season, if you’re lucky, you can witness the famous “mirror” effect, where the ground perfectly reflects the sky. This view was the most amazing natural phenomenon we had ever seen.

The mirror effect

Sunrise

Photoshoot

We had breakfast right on the flats and took some classic tourist photos with the group.

Breakfast with dinosaur

For the final part of our tour, we visited the train cemetery, an open-air museum filled with old, abandoned trains.

Afterward, we arrived in Uyuni and had our last lunch together as a group.

While researching how to get from Uyuni to La Paz, I had read that it’s best to buy bus tickets last minute because road blockades in Bolivia can be unpredictable. There had been a major one back in March that stranded many travelers in Uyuni. When we got to the bus terminal, I started to feel nervous—online, all the night buses to La Paz were sold out. Luckily, we found one company, Trans Titicaca, that still had seats available. The bus was at 9:30 p.m., so we had the entire day to wait.

A British couple from our tour was also taking a different night bus to La Paz, so we spent the day together playing cards, drinking beer, and having dinner. The time passed surprisingly quickly.

We arrived at the bus station around 8:30 p.m., brushed our teeth, and waited at the lane listed on our ticket. At 9:00 p.m., two Trans Titicaca buses pulled into the terminal. Since our ticket said 9:30 p.m. and listed lane 7, we were confident we were in the right place. However, as time passed, one of the buses from the lane next to us departed at 9:15 p.m.

Kaj with the bus we did not get on

Then a random backpacker came up to ask which bus we were taking. When we compared tickets, we realized hers said 10:00 p.m.—and it was for the bus that was now waiting in front of us. That’s when it hit us: our bus had already left.

We rushed to the staff checking in passengers for the next bus, and thankfully, they let us board. But once we found our seats, another woman showed up with the same seat numbers. Kaj went to speak to the staff again, and luckily, there were a few empty seats on the lower level, so they let us take those instead.

It was a very stressful end to a long day.

By coincidence, we ended up sitting next to the same girl who had asked us about the bus earlier, and it turned out she was from Japan. We chatted for a while and helped her out later when we had to pay the “bus terminal tax” — something no one had mentioned beforehand.
entry.summary: ...if you’re lucky, you can witness the famous “mirror” effect, where the ground perfectly reflects the sky.

entry.title: Uyuni day two
entry.content:
Today was a bit more relaxed than yesterdays schedule. We started around 9am to look at the valley of the rocks and then went to the black lagoon. For lunch we went back to the hostal and then loaded our backpacks on the jeep again to drive towards Uyuni. We made a stop a llama farm which was also the guide’s hometown which is now abandoned.

The black lagoon

Llamas

Kaj found a hole

We made one stop at a tourist town where they also make craft beer. This ‘town’ felt very fake although there were some local people living there. We tried some coca beer which tasted like any other beer. A group of small kids pushed a dog into my face which was very unexpected and then we left to drive the rest of the way to our hostel for the night.

Kaj climbed up an old train at the beer place

Our guide made a bonus stop to show us some giant cacti and a quinoa farm.

Rika with Quinoa

Our hostel was very interesting with built in stone beds. Since the weather was too cloudy, we decided to cancel the stargazing part of the tour and had some dinner and went directly to bed.
entry.summary: We tried some coca beer which tasted like any other beer.

entry.title: Uyuni day one
entry.content:
Today was the start of the infamous 2D3N Uyuni tour which consists of crossing the border to Bolivia to go across the salt flats in 4x4 jeep. We were picked up at the hostel at 5am to go straight to the Bolivian border. We had some breakfast there while waiting for the border to open. The view was incredible with the sunrise and the mountains in the background. At breakfast we met 2 other couples (one German/Canadian, one British) whom we would share the jeep and tour with for the next 3 days. We were extremely lucky that they were around the same age group and really friendly, mature and great people.

Sunrise by the border

The border crossing took around an hour as there was a lot of waiting involved. The process was to get one stamp to leave Chile, and then one stamp to enter Bolivia as well as a different checkpoint for customs into Bolivia. I had prepared the QR code for the customs for the both of us, but had accidentally saved the same QR code twice. They still let us in without Kajs QR code so it seemed like the Bolivian border control was not so strict.

Once out of Chile we changed vehicles to the 4x4 and strapped our backpacks to the roof. Inside the jeep it was extremely cramped as we had 6 people plus the driver. The first place headed to was the white lagoon just across the border.

The jeep

The white lagoon

We then had a long drive to the geysers and hot springs at over 4000m elevation. At the geysers it started hauling big chunks of ice suddenly. There were two baths, one a bit warmer than the other around 34 degrees. It felt really nice to soak our tired bodies in the bath.

The thermal baths

After the bath we had some lunch and then heading back in the jeep for a long drive. We stopped at the emerald lagoon and the pink lagoon. It started to be a storm but we also made a stop at some rock formations.

A cool rock formation

We drove a lot this day from 5am to 6pm. We got to our hostel in a tiny village and had some dinner before going to bed.

Hostel room
entry.summary: The border crossing took around an hour as there was a lot of waiting invovled.

entry.title: El Tatio Geysers
entry.content:
Today was our last day in San Pedro. We got up at 4am to go on a tour to the El Tatio Geysers. We drove around 2 hours to get to the geysers which were at an elevation 4320m. We booked this tour last as we were told it’s a good way to acclimatise before the Uyuni tour.

El tatio

We reached the geysers right before the sunrise. It was very cold so the steam could be seen clearly. It made me want to go to the hot springs.

The geysers

After the geysers we went to a village called Machuca to eat breakfast. Then on our way back to San Pedro we visited a lagoon where we saw a herd of vicuñas and flamingos.

Vicuñas by the lagoon

Back at San Pedro we eat some empanadas and did the laundry to prepare for a very early start to Uyuni the following day. We enjoyed our time in the Atacama desert. The scenery was out of this world, and although I do not usually enjoy tours the ones we had were organised extremely well and it was a great experience.
entry.summary: Today was our last day in San Pedro. We got up at 4am to go on a tour to the El Tatio Geysers. We drove around 2 hours to get to the geysers which were at an elevation 4320m. We booked this tour last as we were told it’s a good way to acclimatise before the Uyuni tour.

entry.title: Mountain bikes and meteorites
entry.content:
Today we decided to rent mountain bikes and go to the Garaganta del Diablo (Devil’s throat) ourselves instead of going with an organised tour. We got up bright and early so we could start cycling before it got really hot.

Cycling through through the canyons was so much fun! They were like nothing we’ve seen before, as if taken from some sci-if movie. Twisting and turning through the landscape they guided us over small hills and through tiny caves. At a few points we had to get off our bikes and carry them up a ledge. Finally we got out of the maze and into view came a glorious little mountain. Let’s climb!

At the top of the mountain we could see over the whole valley as well as the volcano in the distance. We ate our packed sandwiches while admiring the view.

On the way back we rode 2 more kilometres to a small church which was quite underwhelming. Kaj had fun splashing through the water while cycling there.

In the evening we visited the Meteorite museum in San Pedro. It was a small museum but very interactive and we were able to touch the meteorites and learn the differences between normal rocks and space rocks from the staff who worked there.
entry.summary: Cycling through through the canyons was so much fun!

entry.title: The lagoons and stargazing
entry.content:
Today we went on a tour to the Baltinache lagoons. There are 7 lagoons in the area which formed from the ocean drying up many years ago. They all have different hues depending on their depth and contain up to 25% salt. There was a sunscreen ban when entering the area as they are trying to preserve the lagoons which have been altered by human activity. Because of this, only one lagoon is allowed for swimming at the moment. Because of the high salt content, you can hardly swim in the lagoon - just float.

The first lagoon which was banned from entry

Another lagoon with salt formations

Floating in the lagoon

After the lagoon tour we rested up a bit before heading to the stargazing tour at around 21:00. We waited for around 40 minutes before the tour guide decided we could get on the van to go to a place less polluted wtih light. The night was very cloudy, but thankfully it had started to clear up. The atacama night sky was really magical. We were first taken to part of the desert to have professional photos taken (which was apparently included in the tour). Afterwards, we moved to another spot to get a lecture on the different constellations and planets. It was weird and beautiful seeing the night sky from the southern hemisphere. We could see Jupiter clearly and the … and the …

Overall, a packed day full of beautiful lagoons and stars! During our Atacama trip we booked a hostel where there is a small kitchen to save money on meals as San Pedro is very expensive. Every meal has been spaghetti with tomato sauce and canned beans.
entry.summary: There are 7 lagoons in the area which formed from the ocean drying up many years ago

entry.title: The Atacama Desert
entry.content:
San Pedro de Atacama is an interesting oasis town. The main industry is tourism and since the desert is so vast, the only easy way to get to the tourist attractions is by booking a tour. The main street in the town is lined with agencies all offering similar tours. Since it was our first day, we decided to take it easy and plan/book our tours for the next 5 days. While we were visitng many agencies comparing the prices, we were suddenly hit with the altitude 2,407 m (7,897 ft) and felt light-headed. It was hard to concentrate and haggle in Spanish, but in the end we managed to book our tours with good prices. The most important one was the 2D3N Uyuni tour which we would need to travel to Bolivia.

We also booked one tour for the evening to the Valley of the Moon. We were in the same van as a Korean tour group - they were very lovely. In this valley there were natural formations that were given meanings such as the ‘Tres Marias’. However, our tour guide was obviously an atheist and preferred looking at the dinosaur-like formation right next to the holy ladies.

The desert

Tres Marias

The dino

We were able to catch the sunset across the vally which was absolutely magical, and the tour also came with free Pisco sours and snacks - which was a great way for people to leave good reveiws right before the tour ends!

Kaj happy with the snacks and cocktails

The Valley of the Moon

Happy sunset!
entry.summary: San Pedro de Atacama is an interesting oasis town.

entry.title: Santiago
entry.content:
Today we left Brazil for Santiago, Chile. We had a very interesting 3 hour flight, as the woman who sat next to us was extremely talkative (could only speak Spanish) and talked to us for the whole duration of the flight. She talked to us about her travels and her family and it was a good chance to practice Spanish. She gave us some tips for Santiago, and also talked about how the city has changed due to the Columbian and Venezuelan immigrants…It turned out she was very conservative and also kept asking Rika when she will have a baby. In the end she even gave us a curtain that she had crocheted! Thank you Laura!

After landing in Santiago we got an Uber to our hotel. Apparently uber isn’t allowed around the airport but we were able to get one anyway. The drivers seem to be coordinating where it’s “safe” to go using radio communication with other drivers to avoid inspection.

We arrived safely at the hotel and found a vegan restaurant a few blocks away. We tried the famous Chilean hot-dog, “Completo” and a pizza. We were super hungry at this point so even though the pizza was huge we finished it all. It wasn’t the best pizza but the hot-dog was good.

Completo

Satisfied with our meal we went back to the hotel and went to sleep early!

The following day was the day of our frlight to the Atacama desert. It was planned for the afternoon, though, so we decided to hike up San Cristobal mountain in the morning. It was close to our hotel and it was a nice climb that took maybe an hour and a half. We were able to find a “shortcut” through the forest which was very fun!

The hill

Can you tell we are in Chile?

The view from the top

We headed down and got lunch at El Vegan. We got a mushroom empanada and a Pastel de Choclo which were both excellent! The pastel is like a sweet corn-pie with a savoury “meat”-sauce underneath. Really nice.

Finishing off our meal we went to the airport to head to our next destination: The Atacama Desert.
entry.summary: Today we left Brazil for Santiago, Chile.

entry.title: Iguazú Falls (Argentinian side)
entry.content:
The falls are a natural border between Brazil and Argentina, but 80% of the falls lie on Argentinian side. This meant that we could not leave the area without also visiting the national park on the Argentinian side.

We were picked up at the hotel at 6:40am and headed to the border after making a few more stops at nearby hotels. At the border, we had to wait around 30 minutes for the Argentinian border police to check through our passports. The driver explained that there is a new agreement between Brazil and Argentina that if the tourists are making a day trip to the falls, they do not need a stamp from the Brazilian border police when exiting the country. This made the whole process a lot quick than expected, and we managed to arrive at the park at around 9:00am.

The Brazilian side of the falls was more of a panoramic view compared to the Argentinian side where you could go right above, below and between the waterfall system. There are three main routes that take you around the park. If you complete them you get an abundance of views and in contrast to the Brazilian side you get really close to the waterfalls.

The first route, Circuita Inferior, took us through the rainforest at a low elevation, giving us dramatic views of the falls from below. We got some company from some very cute coatis on this trail! (Rika thinks that Argentinian coatis are cuter than Brazilian ones.)

After completing the first route we continued on to the Circuita Superior, which brings you right to on top of the waterfalls. Walking mostly on footpaths we were able to see the falls up close. You’re walking over the river which is the source for the falls and it is quite beautiful with clear water and fish swimming around.

It was approaching 11 am and we headed back to a sort of rest area where we encountered some monkeys! They were trying to get to the food, the opportunistic scoundrels, but we escaped into a cage specifically designed to keep monkeys and coatis out. After hastily finishing our packed lunch we boarded a train for the main attraction of the day: La Garganta del Diablo!

The train ride took about 15 minutes and brought us to the start of a kilometer-long foot-path over the river. Trekking it took about 20 minutes with a couple of stops for photos. The amount of tourists on this thing was rather remarkable but everything went along relatively smoothly.

At the end of the foot path a spectacular experience awaited: the biggest waterfall in Iguazu Falls, known as La Garganta del Diablo, or the Devli’s Throat. Approaching it you see a cloud of white eater particles arising fron where the bottom of the waterfall and by the time you reach the end of the path you’re wet. You’re now right above the torrent and the sound is awesome. 1.5 million liters of water crashes down 80 meters, creating an opaque mist totally conceiling the bottom of the fall. The sound and the splashing of water and the neverending flow of water make for a unique experience to say the least.

After wrestling other tourists for the best selfie spot for about 30 minutes we headed back along the foot-path, happy that we’d experienced one of the true naturals wonders of this world.
entry.summary: Exploring the Argentinian side of the falls.

entry.title: Iguazu Falls (Brazilian side)
entry.content:
The second day we got up early and had breakfast at the hotel. Brazilian breakfast always has plenty of fresh fruit which is very nice :)

After breakfast we got an uber to the Parque Nacional do Iguzu, which has views of the waterfalls from the brazilian side. Before that though we decided to pay a visit to Parque do Aves (park of birds) which is a bird sanctuary located right next to the entrance to the national park.

In the bird park they keep and breed many endangered species of birds found in the atlantic rainforest (which is different from Amazonas). Deforestation adn climate change has resulted in the extinction of many species and even more are at risk.

To avoid the overpriced tourist food inside the national park, we brought our own lunch of flatbreads, hummus, fruit and nuts. After a quick lunch in the shade, we finally headed to Iguazu national park.

The tickets for the national park were based on a sepcific entrance time, so there was hardly any queue when we got there. We waited for an open deck shuttle bus to take us to the start of the trail to the falls.

The trail to the first look of the falls took around 10 minutes. It is hard to describe how it felt to see the falls for the first time. It looked like something out of this world.

We also ran into some wild coatis on the trail. The name ‘coati’ comes from the Tupi native language of Brazil. It means “belt-nose” and is in reference to the coati’s sleeping positing - curled up with its snout tucked in its belly.

The best part was that as we continued through the trail, we realized that the falls that we had seen was just a tiny part of the waterfall system.

Under the main fall it was almost raining on us.

At the end of the trail, we got onto the shuttle bus that would take us back to the entrance. While on the bus we heard an announcement that the next stop would have a 2.8km hiking trail called San Jose. We made an impromptu decision to get off at the station and do the trail as we had some time to spare before the park closed for the day.

The hike turned out to be a pretty narrow trail with no other people in sight. At the start there was a sign on how to react when coming across a jaguar. It definitely set the mood for the hike :D

The hike took 1 hour and it had so many beautiful viewing points also with smaller waterfalls. The only thing we missed out on was being able to swim in the nature as the water level was too high and there were signs to not enter.

After a very dangerous uber ride back to the town, we took a dip in the pool and then headed out for dinner.

For some reason, the town of Foz de Iguazu has a lot of Shawarma restaurants. We decided to try one of them and ordered a veggie meat patty plate with bean sauce, fries and salad. It was very very tasty.
